skill.name: gitlab-ci-patterns
skill.description: Build GitLab CI/CD pipelines with multi-stage workflows, caching, and distributed runners for scalable automation. Use when implementing GitLab CI/CD, optimizing pipeline performance, or setting up automated testing and deployment.
skill.summary: Create GitLab CI/CD pipeline patterns for builds, tests, deployments, caching, and security scans.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged shell command patterns, hardcoded URLs, and weak cryptography indicators in SKILL.md. Review found the command and URL detections are Markdown GitLab CI examples, and the weak cryptography detections are false positives, but two CI examples recommend insecure operational patterns. No prompt injection or malicious exfiltration intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Insecure Kubernetes TLS Verification Example
medium_findings.description: The deployment example disables TLS verification when configuring a Kubernetes cluster. This is risky if copied into production because it weakens server identity checks, although the skill does not execute the command itself.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The insecure option is present in a deployment command example. Confidence is high because the line clearly weakens TLS verification, but the risk is limited to user adoption of the documented pattern.

medium_findings.title: Registry Password Passed Through Command Argument
medium_findings.description: The Docker login example passes the registry password as a command argument. This can expose secrets through shell history, process listings, or logs; safer patterns use standard input or GitLab-supported authentication helpers.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line clearly places a password variable in a command argument. It is documentation rather than executed code, so the impact depends on whether users copy it into pipelines.

low_findings.title: False Positive External Command Detections
low_findings.description: The static Ruby or shell backtick detections are inside Markdown text and fenced GitLab CI YAML examples. They document pipeline commands but are not executable skill code, so no direct command execution path was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content appears in documentation or fenced YAML blocks, and no separate executable script file exists. The package contains only SKILL.md, which supports the false positive assessment.

low_findings.title: False Positive Hardcoded URL Detections
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are placeholder environment URLs in GitLab CI examples. They point to example.com domains and do not indicate data exfiltration or live network calls from the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL locations are environment metadata examples rather than network request code. The use of example.com strongly indicates placeholder documentation.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Detections
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography flags do not correspond to cryptographic code or hashing operations. The referenced lines are descriptive skill text and related skill names, so no weak cryptographic algorithm use was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no cryptographic API, hash algorithm, or encryption workflow at the flagged lines. The finding appears to be triggered by ordinary words in documentation.

dangerous_patterns.title: TLS Verification Disabled in Example Command
dangerous_patterns.description: A Kubernetes setup example disables TLS verification. This pattern should be replaced with certificate validation guidance before broad publication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact deployment option is present in the example and is a recognized insecure configuration. It is not malicious because it is not executed by the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secret Passed as Docker CLI Argument
dangerous_patterns.description: A Docker login example passes a password variable through a command argument. This pattern can leak secrets and should use a safer input method.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The example clearly uses a password variable in the command argument position. The issue is limited to documentation quality unless users copy the pattern.

content.value_statement: GitLab CI/CD setup often becomes hard to maintain as builds, tests, and deployments grow. This skill provides practical pipeline patterns for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code users who need structured automation guidance.
actual_capabilities: Explains multi-stage GitLab CI pipeline structure for build, test, and deploy jobs.
Provides Docker build and registry push patterns for GitLab Container Registry.
Shows staging and production deployment patterns using GitLab environments.
Includes Terraform validate, plan, and apply pipeline stages.
Covers GitLab security scanning templates and a Trivy container scan job.
Describes cache, artifact, and dynamic child pipeline patterns.
limitations: Does not include the referenced resource files in this package.
Does not validate generated YAML against a live GitLab instance.
Requires users to adapt image names, namespaces, variables, and credentials.
Contains examples that should be hardened before production use.

use_cases.title: Create a New Application Pipeline
use_cases.description: Use the skill to draft build, test, artifact, cache, and deployment stages for a GitLab project.

use_cases.title: Standardize Deployment Workflows
use_cases.description: Use the skill to compare staging and production deployment patterns with manual production gates.

use_cases.title: Add Infrastructure Automation
use_cases.description: Use the skill to outline Terraform validation, planning, and controlled apply stages in GitLab CI.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Basic Pipeline
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a single application
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GitLab CI pipeline for my application. Include build, test, cache, artifacts, and a manual production deploy stage.

prompt_templates.title: Improve an Existing Pipeline
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate optimization of current CI jobs
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my GitLab CI pipeline and suggest improvements for stage order, caching, artifacts, and runner efficiency.

prompt_templates.title: Design Multi-Environment Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a GitLab CI deployment workflow for staging and production. Include environment tracking, manual approval, and secure variable usage.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Platform Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GitLab CI strategy that combines Terraform validation, Docker image publishing, GitLab security scanning, and Kubernetes deployment controls.

output_examples.input: I need a GitLab CI pipeline for a Node application.
output_examples.output: The skill would outline build, test, and deploy stages, recommend dependency caching, and describe artifact handling for the build output.

output_examples.input: I want staging and production deployments from GitLab.
output_examples.output: The skill would propose separate staging and production jobs, environment metadata, branch rules, and a manual approval gate for production.

output_examples.input: I need Terraform in GitLab CI.
output_examples.output: The skill would describe validate, plan, and apply stages, including an artifact handoff from planning to a controlled apply job.

best_practices: Replace placeholder domains, namespaces, and deployment names with project-specific values.
Use protected and masked GitLab CI/CD variables for credentials and tokens.
Harden security-sensitive examples before production, especially registry login and Kubernetes TLS configuration.
anti_patterns: Do not copy deployment examples into production without reviewing secret handling and TLS settings.
Do not use broad cache keys when jobs require isolated dependency or build caches.
Do not run production deployment or infrastructure apply jobs without manual approval controls.

faq.question: Does this skill create complete GitLab CI files?
faq.answer: It provides patterns and example structures that can be adapted into a project-specific GitLab CI file.

faq.question: Can it help with Docker image publishing?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a Docker build and push pattern for GitLab Container Registry workflows.

faq.question: Does it cover Kubernetes deployment?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes kubectl-based deployment examples for staging and production environments.

faq.question: Does it include security scanning?
faq.answer: Yes. It references GitLab SAST, dependency scanning, container scanning, and a Trivy scan job.

faq.question: Can it validate my pipeline in GitLab?
faq.answer: No. It can guide structure and review, but validation requires GitLab CI linting or a real pipeline run.

faq.question: Is the skill ready for production use?
faq.answer: Use it as a starting point. Review secrets, permissions, runner settings, and deployment security before production.
